title: Home incident command board
summary: A complete offline workflow for home incident command board, designed for households, small groups, and community helpers during disruption.
content:
Purpose: Home incident command board turns one visible board for decisions, people, risks, tasks, supplies, and next checks into a step-by-step offline routine that can be followed when people are tired, stressed, or without internet.

Use this record as a full solution, not as a single trick. Prepare the materials before the emergency, run the steps in order during the incident, write down decisions, and stop when the situation requires official responders, trained professionals, or evacuation.

Best use: small households, apartment buildings, villages, shelters, and volunteer groups that need a visible method for command board without improvising under pressure.
materials: one paper checklist labeled 'Home incident command board'
pen, tape, and a visible place for the command board notes
small bag or box holding the tools needed for home incident command board
contact list for family, neighbors, local authorities, and emergency services relevant to command board
dated log sheet for decisions, times, quantities, and problems found during home incident command board
backup option prepared in advance in case the main method for home incident command board fails
steps: Start by making the area safe enough to think: remove immediate life threats, move children and vulnerable people away from the work area, and call 112 or local emergency services if someone is in danger. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Home incident command board".

Define the exact problem in one sentence: what happened, where it is, who is affected, what is getting worse, and what must not be touched. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Home incident command board".
Assign one calm person to lead home incident command board, one person to record decisions, and one person to watch for new hazards instead of everyone acting at once.
Gather only the materials listed for command board so the team does not waste time searching or carrying unnecessary items.

Run the safest reversible action first; avoid breaking, forcing, mixing chemicals, entering damaged areas, or using power tools unless the record specifically allows it. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Home incident command board".

Check the result after each action: did the risk reduce, did a new risk appear, and does anyone need rest, water, medicine, shade, warmth, or evacuation? Record-specific context: apply this line to "Home incident command board".
Write down time, names, quantities, locations, and decisions so another person can continue the process without guessing. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Home incident command board".
Close the routine by marking what is safe, what is unsafe, what must be checked again, and when the next review of home incident command board is due.
warnings: Do not let home incident command board delay evacuation, medical care, fire response, flood avoidance, or official instructions.

Stop immediately if the task exposes people to smoke, gas smell, live electricity, moving water, unstable structures, chemicals, violence, or severe heat/cold. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Home incident command board".

Do not assign children, exhausted people, or medically fragile people to heavy, risky, or night-time tasks unless it is the only safe option and they are supervised. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Home incident command board".

Avoid anonymous advice, rumors, social-media claims, and improvised repairs when official guidance or a qualified professional is available. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Home incident command board".
common_mistakes: Trying to solve home incident command board alone instead of using a small visible team.
Skipping written notes, which causes repeated work, arguments, or dangerous assumptions later.

Using the first available tool or container without checking contamination, damage, sharp edges, fire risk, or electrical risk. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Home incident command board".

Treating comfort problems as life-threatening while missing quiet urgent needs such as medicine, dehydration, infants, older adults, or disabled people. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Home incident command board".
alternatives: If the full method is too slow, use the first three steps only: make safe, define the problem, and assign a recorder.

If supplies are missing, replace the equipment with a labeled paper note, a safe boundary, and a scheduled recheck rather than improvising a risky tool. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Home incident command board".
If the household cannot manage command board, escalate early to neighbors, building management, local authorities, clinic, fire service, or 112 depending on the hazard.
failure_signs: People disagree about what is safe because the status of home incident command board is not written or visible.
The same problem returns after the first action, showing that the cause was not controlled.
Someone becomes dizzy, confused, short of breath, overheated, very cold, injured, or unable to continue.

New hazards appear: smell of gas, smoke, contaminated water, unstable walls, electrical sparks, aggressive behavior, or official evacuation order. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Home incident command board".
when_not_to_use: Do not use this routine as a substitute for emergency services, medical diagnosis, structural inspection, utility repair, or fire/flood rescue. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Home incident command board".
Do not use it inside a building, road, shelter, or landscape that authorities have declared unsafe or closed.
Do not use it to justify hoarding, secrecy, forced labor, discrimination, or ignoring vulnerable people.
short_term: For the first hours, use home incident command board to stabilize decisions, reduce confusion, and protect people before protecting objects.
long_term: For longer disruption, turn home incident command board into a daily checklist with names, times, stock levels, repairs needed, and lessons learned.
if_method_fails: If home incident command board fails, stop the routine, isolate the hazard if safe, record what changed, move people to the safest available place, and escalate to official or trained help.
environment_notes: Adapt home incident command board to apartment blocks, villages, islands, heat, cold, smoke, floodwater, limited transport, disabled access, pets, and local authority instructions.

failure_signs: People disagree about what is safe because the status of emergency lessons-learned log is not written or visible.
The same problem returns after the first action, showing that the cause was not controlled.
Someone becomes dizzy, confused, short of breath, overheated, very cold, injured, or unable to continue.

New hazards appear: smell of gas, smoke, contaminated water, unstable walls, electrical sparks, aggressive behavior, or official evacuation order. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Emergency lessons-learned log".
when_not_to_use: Do not use this routine as a substitute for emergency services, medical diagnosis, structural inspection, utility repair, or fire/flood rescue. Record-specific context: apply this line to "Emergency lessons-learned log".
Do not use it inside a building, road, shelter, or landscape that authorities have declared unsafe or closed.
Do not use it to justify hoarding, secrecy, forced labor, discrimination, or ignoring vulnerable people.
short_term: For the first hours, use emergency lessons-learned log to stabilize decisions, reduce confusion, and protect people before protecting objects.
long_term: For longer disruption, turn emergency lessons-learned log into a daily checklist with names, times, stock levels, repairs needed, and lessons learned.
if_method_fails: If emergency lessons-learned log fails, stop the routine, isolate the hazard if safe, record what changed, move people to the safest available place, and escalate to official or trained help.
environment_notes: Adapt emergency lessons-learned log to apartment blocks, villages, islands, heat, cold, smoke, floodwater, limited transport, disabled access, pets, and local authority instructions.
